model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.author: Abuzeid Ibrahim
model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.message: Great tutorial, but Could you explain to me how you mocked the ProductDetailView,Where are you came from with ProductDetailViewSpy??

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message:
Hi Abuzeid Ibrahim, thank you for reading my post!! 😊I added the implementation of the ProductDetailViewSpy class in the article. You can find also its implementation on the repository with the code contained in this article (if you want to try it out on your mac):

https://github.com/chicio/M...

Basically it is a class that implements the View interface (like the UIViewController of the app), and uses some property to keep track of the fact that a method has been called. So for the showTitleHasBeenCalled will be true when the show(title aTitle: String) function has been called. In this way you can test the interaction between the view and the presenter.
I had to do this because (maybe as you know) Swift doesn't have a real mocking framework (due to the fact that reflection has limited support and subclassing of NSProxy classes isn't allow (so no proxy class like other framework OCMock, Mockito...) ). So in Swift generally you write your own Mock/Spy in the way I shown you in this article. In the last time some alternatives has been created, like some mock-alike framework like this one

https://github.com/Brightif...

but honestly I didn't try it yet.
I hope you find the post helpful and continue to read my blog!! 😊

model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.author: Usman
model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.message: Could you please pass through dependencies rather that creating those inside the classes?

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message:
Hi @Usman, first of all thank you for reading my post!! 🙂 The main objective of the post is to show the “model view presenter” architecture to the readers. This is why I kept everything as simple as possible. So I instantiated all the dependencies in the view controllers. Anyway it is possible to use a dependencies injection framework to get all you repositories, presenters and so on (and you must do it 😌). You can check the following dependency injection library available for swift:

https://github.com/Swinject...
https://github.com/square/C...

Let me know if you need more help 🙂.

model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.author: Varun Mehta
model-view-presenter-architecture-ios-sw.message:
Great Post!! Well articulated.
Just wanted to make one improvement. I see a retain cycle between ProductsPresenter and ProductsViewController. So either of productsView property in ProductsPresenter OR productsPresenter in ProductsViewController should be taken as weak.

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message:
Hi @Varun Mehta, thank you for reading my post!!😊
You're right: in the previous implementation there was a retain cycle between the controllers and the presenters. I created the post and the example during a night of coding/studying so I missed that bug 😁. When I create a presenter in my daily job I usually set the view on the presenter as unowned. In this way I don't need to unwrap it each time I access to it (and I also know for sure that the UIViewController that acts as the presenter view will be the final object to be deallocated).
So a big thank you for you bug report!!! Now you can see the github repo code and the article above fixed 😎.

kotlin-junit5-mockk.author: Paolo Sciarra
kotlin-junit5-mockk.message:
Hi Fabrizio,
great article. I have just somes ideas I want to share with you: Mockk requires you to instruct your mocks otherwise it will fail because it received an unknown call and it doesn't know what to do with that.

I tend to do this way: when I am mocking a query a method that returns some value, I will do as you did also being specific about the parameters used to get the result for the query, when I am mocking a command, some action I have to do and I need to verify it was executed, I am less specific in the `every` block and I am very specific in the `verify` block.

Also I drop entirely the verify block for the mocked query because I am more interested in the value I receive then the fact I actually called that collaborator.

In this way I find the test failures are easier to understand.

What do you think?

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message:
Hi @Paolo Sciarra ,

thank you fo your feedback!! ♥️ For the first part:

when I am mocking a command, some action I have to do and I need to verify it was executed, I am less specific in the `every` block and I am very specific in the `verify` block.

Very interesting, I will try this approach as it seems a good strategy to have a real feedback about what call you're expecting and, as you already said, it would make the test failure error more readable (because you will not see the one throw when a mock is missing).

For the second part:

Also I drop entirely the verify block for the mocked query because I am more interested in the value I receive then the fact I actually called that collaborator.

Based on what I know and I remember about MockK, this library doesn't throw an error if you have the mock instructed in the test but you then you call it with other parameters (this is different with respect to other library like Mockito). This is why I always add a verify block (and again, e.g. in Mockito I was used to setup just the mocked query). But maybe this is something that was happening only in the first versions of the library, so I will check again soon 😎

dynamic-imports-webpack-chunks.author: Iftikhar Ali
dynamic-imports-webpack-chunks.message: ES6 provides <script type="module" ..=""></script> which can download other modules on the fly. Will webpack dynamic import still be relevant with that in place?

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message: Hi @Iftikhar Ali , thank you for your question. I think web pack dynamic imports are still relevant, because they let you code split your code base in an automatic way without worrying about optimising the modules imports and compilation. Basically it is a "configuration" approach that frees you from the burden of tuning code splitting.

id3tageditor-swift-read-write-id3-tag-mp.author: SouthernYankee
id3tageditor-swift-read-write-id3-tag-mp.message: Thanks for this framework. I recently discovered that one cannot use the Swift API's to modify and save .mp3 metadata. Frustrating! But, your framework has helped me. Thank you!

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message: Thank you for your support @SouthernYankee. Remember to ⭐️ star ⭐️ my repo in order to support my open source work

id3tageditor-swift-read-write-id3-tag-mp.author: SouthernYankee
id3tageditor-swift-read-write-id3-tag-mp.message: I have mp3 files that have an attached picture, but iTunes/Music does not read the artwork. Is this because the picture type is incorrect in the tag? If so, how would I use your framework to change the picture type to be front cover?

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message: The general thing I suggest is to update/set the id3 tag of your mp3 to version 3 and play a little bit with the Attached picture frame contained in my framework. At the time I wrote this article iTunes supported only attached picture frame with "other" set as type. You can try to set it with my framework to different types and see how iTunes behave. I hope this can help you!!

scene-kit-physically-based-rendering.author: Joice George
scene-kit-physically-based-rendering.message: can we set the position of a node related to another node other than parent? Thank you

replies.author: Fabrizio
replies.message: Hi@Joice George, the position represented in this article for the Object class is a SCNVector3. So as long as you pass a vector (that could be also the position of another object that is not strictly the parent) you can set the position as you want.
